feedback.text: The file I attached shows how far things have slipped when it comes to what we actually ownespecially in gaming. We used to have real standards. When you bought a game, it was yours. Now, with how things are going, we're losing that. Companies can pull access, shut things down, or change the rules anytime, and were left with nothing to show for it. We need proper protections. If I buy a game, I should have the right to play it whenever I want. But most of the time now, the Terms of Service or EULA basically say they can take it away whenever they feel like it. Thats not ownership thats renting, and it should be called what it is. This goes for more than just games, too. We need to protect access to things like our payment processors and bank accounts. Losing access to those over vague rules or automated decisions is dangerous, and theres no real accountability when it happens. Another big one is our data. Companies make a lot of money off our personal data, especially from advertising. If they are selling it, we deserve a cut at least 50 to 70%. Its our data. Without us, they wouldnt have anything to sell in the first place. And its time we start using honest labels. If somethings a subscription, call it that. If its a rental, say so. Dont sell something like its a permanent product if it can be taken away at any time. There needs to be clear wording in Terms of Service and EULAs that spells this out. Right now, everything is written in a way that favors the company and leaves the customer with nothing if things go wrong. At the end of the day, were not asking for anything crazy. we just want to go back to a system where what you buy is yours, your data is respected, and the rules are fair.
